elements.sent-2411-001: Weight sharing, equivariance, and local filters, as in convolutional neural networks, are believed to contribute to the sample efficiency of neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, it is not clear how each one of these design choices contribute to the generalization error.
elements.sent-2411-003: Through the lens of statistical learning theory, we aim to provide an insight into this question by characterizing the relative impact of each choice on the sample complexity.
elements.sent-2411-004: We obtain lower and upper sample complexity bounds for a class of single hidden layer networks.
elements.sent-2411-005: It is shown that the gain of equivariance is directly manifested in the bound, while getting a similar increase for weight sharing depends on the sharing mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-006: Among our results, we obtain a completely dimension-free bound for equivariant networks for a class of pooling operations.
elements.sent-2411-007: We show that the bound depends merely on the norm of filters, which is tighter than using the spectral norm of the respective matrix.
elements.sent-2411-008: We also characterize the trade-off in sample complexity between the parametrization of filters in spatial and frequency domains, particularly when spatial filters are localized as in vanilla convolutional neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-009: In recent years, equivariant neural networks have gained particular interest within the machine learning community thanks to their inherent ability to preserve certain transformations in the input data, thereby providing a form of inductive bias that aligns with many real-world problems.
elements.sent-2411-010: Indeed, equivariant networks have shown remarkable performance in various applications, ranging from computer vision to molecular chemistry, where data often exhibit specific forms of symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-011: The convolution operation in \acp{CNN ensures that the response to a particular feature is the same, regardless of its spatial location in the input data.}
elements.sent-2411-012: However, the concept of equivariance extends beyond just translation, accommodating a broader spectrum of transformations such as rotations, reflections, and scaling, among others.
elements.sent-2411-013: The inductive bias introduced by equivariance, in the form of symmetry preservation, offers an intuitive connection to their generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-014: By encoding prior knowledge about the structure of the data, equivariant networks can efficiently exploit the inherent symmetries, reducing sample complexity and improving generalization performance.
elements.sent-2411-015: This ability to generalize from a limited set of examples is crucial to their success.
elements.sent-2411-016: Their mathematical foundation is grounded in group representation theory, which provides a powerful framework to understand and design neural networks that are equivariant or invariant to the action of a group of transformations.
elements.sent-2411-017: Besides the inductive bias of data symmetry, the generalization benefits of \acp{CNN and \acp{GCNN are attributed additionally to the weight-sharing, implemented by the convolution operation, and the locality, implemented by the smaller filter size.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In this paper, we study the impact of equivariance, weight-sharing, and locality on generalization within the framework of statistical learning theory.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our focus will be on neural networks with one hidden layer.
elements.sent-2411-020: Similar to works like \cite{vardi_sample_2022, magen_initialization-dependent_2023 , we believe that this study provides a first step toward a better understanding of deeper networks.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Getting dimension-free generalization error bounds constitutes an important line of research in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-022: Following this line of work, we provide various dimension-free and norm-based bounds for one hidden layer networks.
elements.sent-2411-023: See Appendix \ref{app:learning_theory_goals for discussions on the desiderata of learning theory.}
elements.sent-2411-024: We consider a class of one hidden layer networks where the first layer is a multi-channel equivariant layer followed by point-wise non-linearity, a pooling layer, and the final linear layer.
elements.sent-2411-025: We assume that the $\ell_2$-norm of the parameters of each layer is bounded.
elements.sent-2411-026: For architectures based on group convolution with point-wise non-linearity, we provide generalization bounds for various pooling operations that are completely dimension-free.
elements.sent-2411-027: We obtain a similar norm-based for general equivariant networks.
elements.sent-2411-028: We also provide a lower bound on Rademacher complexity that shows the tightness of our bound.
elements.sent-2411-029: We show that no gain is observed if the analysis is conducted for the networks parameterized in the frequency domain.
elements.sent-2411-030: When the equivariant layer is replaced by a layer with, not necessarily equivariant, weight-sharing, we provide as well a dimension-free bound.
elements.sent-2411-031: By studying the bound, it can be seen that some particular weight-sharing schemes, although not all of them, can provide similar generalization guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-032: Next, we provide another bound for networks with local filters and show that the locality can bring additional gain on top of equivariance.
elements.sent-2411-033: We will then show that there is a trade-off between locality in the spatial domain and frequency domain, important for band-limited inputs.
elements.sent-2411-034: The trade-off is characterized by the uncertainty principle.
elements.sent-2411-035: Finally, we provide the numerical verification of our bounds.
elements.sent-2411-036: The generalization bounds are all obtained using Rademacher complexity analysis.
elements.sent-2411-037: We relegate all proofs to the appendix.
elements.sent-2411-038: We introduce some of the notations used throughout the paper.
elements.sent-2411-040: The term $\norm{\cdot}$ refers to the $\ell_2$-norm, which for the space of matrices is the Frobenius norm.
elements.sent-2411-041: The spectral norm of a matrix $\mA$ is denoted by $\norm{\mA _{2\to 2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-042: A positive homoegeneous activation function $\sigma(\cdots)$ is a function that satisfies $\sigma(\lambda x) = \lambda\sigma(x)$ for all $\lambda\geq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-043: The loss functions are assumed to be $1$-Lipschitz.
elements.sent-2411-044: The matrix of the training data is denoted by $\mX = [\vx_1 \dots \vx_m]$.

elements.sent-2411-045: We first introduce key concepts from group and representation theories necessary to present our main results.
elements.sent-2411-046: In the rest of this work, we will generally assume a compact group $G$.
elements.sent-2411-047: Note that this includes any finite group.
elements.sent-2411-048: For the most part, we will consider Abelian groups, i.e., commutative groups (such as planar rotations or periodic translations).
elements.sent-2411-049: Given two spaces $\mathcal{X , \mathcal{Y}$ carrying an action of a group $G$, a function $\phi: \mathcal{X \to \mathcal{Y}$ is said to be equivariant with respect to $G$ if $\phi(g.x) = g.\phi(x)$ for any $x \in \mathcal{X}$, i.e. if $\phi$ commutes with the group's action.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: For example, the spaces $\mathcal{X , \mathcal{Y}$ could be vector spaces, and the group action $.: G \times \mathcal{X \to \mathcal{X}$ could be a linear function.}}
elements.sent-2411-051: If $G$ is a finite group, the most popular design choice to construct equivariant networks relies on the group convolution operator \cite{cohen_group_2016 , thereby generalizing typical convolutional neural networks (CNNs).}
elements.sent-2411-052: Specifically, given an input signal $x: G \to \R$ over $G$ and a filter $w: G \to \R$, the group convolution produces another output signal $y: G \to \R$ defined as: \begin{equation}y(g) = (w \conv_G x)(g) := \sum_{h \in G} w(g^{-1}h) x(h) \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-053: Note that the signals $x, w, y$ can be represented as vectors $\vx, \vw, \vy \in \R^{|G|}$; hence, the convolution operation can be expressed as $\vy = \mW \vx$, where $\mW \in \R^{|G| \times |G|}$ is a group-circulant matrix encoding $G$-convolution with the filter $\vw$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Like in classical CNNs, one typically considers multi-channel input signals $x: G \to \R^{c_0}$, filters $w: G \to \R^{c_1 \times c_0}$ and output signals $y: G \to \R^{c_1}$.
elements.sent-2411-055: We represent a multi-channel signal $x: G \to \R^{c_0}$ as a stack of features over the group $G$, namely, a tensor $(\vx(1),\dots,\vx({c_0 ))$ of shape $|G|\times c_0$ (i.e. with $c_0$ channels and each $\vx(i) \in \R^{|G|}$).}
elements.sent-2411-056: Then, a convolution layer consists of $c_1$ convolutional filters $\{\vw_j, j\in [c_1]\}$, each of size $|G|\times c_0$, parametrized by per-channel convolutions $\vw_{(j,i)}\in \R^{|G| , i\in[c_0]$; the convolution operation, which yields the output channel $j$, is given by \begin{equation}\vy{(j)} = \sum_{i=1}^{c_0}\vw_{(j,i)} \conv_G \vx{(i)} \in \R^{|G|}, j\in [c_1]\end{equation} In summary, a group convolution layer can be visualized as \begin{equation}0.65{$ \overbrace{ \left[ \begin{array}{c|c|c|c} \mW_{(1,1)} \mW_{(2,1)} \dots \mW_{(c_{0}, 1)} \mW_{(1,2)} \mW_{(2,2)} \dots \mW_{(c_{0}, 2)} \vdots \vdots \ddots \vdots \mW_{(1,c_1)} \mW_{(2,c_1)} \dots \mW_{(c_{0}, c_1)} \end{array} \right] }^{ \textstyle\begin{array}{c} \mW \end{array} } \cdot \overbrace{ \begin{bmatrix} \vx(1) \in \R^{|G|} \vx(2) \in \R^{|G|} \vdots \vx(c_{0}) \in \R^{|G|} \end{bmatrix} }^{ \textstyle\begin{array}{c} \vx \end{array} } = \overbrace{ \begin{bmatrix} \vy(1) \in \R^{|G|} \vy(2) \in \R^{|G|} \vdots \vy(c_1) \in \R^{|G|} \end{bmatrix} }^{ \textstyle\begin{array}{c} \vy \end{array} } $} \label{eq:equivariant_layer}\end{equation} Here, any $\mW_{(i, j) \in \R^{\card{G \times \card{G }$ block is a group circulant matrix corresponding to $G$, which encodes a $G$-convolution parameterised by a filter $\vw_{i, j \in \R^{\card{G }$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Note that the output of the convolution is a $\card{G}\times c_1$ signal, i.e. it contains a different value $y(g, j)$ for each group element $g \in G$ and output channel $j \in [c_1]$.
elements.sent-2411-058: Note also that the group $G$ naturally acts on a signal $x: G \to \R^c$ as $g: x \mapsto g.x$, with $[g.x](h) = x(g^{-1 h)$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Then, one can show that the group convolution operator above is equivariant with respect to this action on its input and output, i.e. $w \conv_G g.x = g.(w \conv_G x)$.
elements.sent-2411-060: Additionally, it is well known, e.g. \cite{generaltheory, Kondor2018-GENERAL , that group convolutions with learnable filters in this form parameterize the most general linear equivariant maps between feature spaces of signals over a compact group $G$.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Finally, \ac{GCNN typically uses an activation function applied point-wise on the convolution output.}
elements.sent-2411-062: In our 1-layer architecture, a first convolution layer is followed by a pointwise activation layer and, then, a per-channel - average or max - pooling layer, which produces $c_1$ invariant features.
elements.sent-2411-063: These final features are mixed with the final linear layer $\vu$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The final network is then given as: \begin{equation}h_{\vu,\vw}(\vx)\defeq \vu^\top P\circ{\sigma { \begin{pmatrix} \sum_{i=1}^{c_0}\vw_{(1,i)} \conv_G \vx{(i)}) \vdots \sum_{i=1}^{c_0}\vw_{(c_1,i)} \conv_G \vx{(i)}) \end{pmatrix} } } \label{def:gcnn_1layer}\end{equation} where $P(\cdot)$ is the pooling operation, and $\vw\defeq (\vw_{(j,i) )_{j\in[c_1],i\in[c_0]}$ is the concatenation of all kernels.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Previous works attempted to improve machine learning models by leveraging prior knowledge about the symmetries and the geometry of a problem \cite{bronstein2021geometric .}
elements.sent-2411-067: A variety of design strategies have been explored in the literature to achieve group equivariance, for example via equivariant MLPs \cite{Shawe-Taylor_1993,Shawe-Taylor_1989,finzi2021practical , group convolution \cite{cohen_group_2016,Kondor2018-GENERAL, bekkers2018roto , Lie group convolution \cite{bekkers2020bspline, finzi_generalizing_2020 , steerable convolution \cite{cohen_steerable_2016,generaltheory, Worrall2017-HNET,Weiler2018-STEERABLE,3d_steerableCNNs,TensorFieldNets, Weiler2019, NEURIPS2020_15231a7c,brandstetter2021geometric,cesa2022a and, very recently, by using geometric algebra \cite{ruhe2023clifford, brehmer2023geometric , to only mention a few.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Some of these ideas have also been used to generalize convolution beyond groups to more generic manifolds via the framework of Gauge equivariance \cite{cohen2019gauge, weiler_coordinate_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-070: While equivariance is generally considered a powerful inductive bias leading to improved data efficiency, this large selection of equivariant designs raises the following question: what impact different architectural choices have on performance and to what extent do they aid generalization?.

elements.sent-2411-073: Later, \cite{elesedy_provably_2021 use VC-dimension analysis and derive more concrete bounds.}

elements.paragraph-2411-008: Generalization bounds for Convolutional neural networks
elements.sent-2411-083: As one of the most popular deep learning architectures, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have received particular attention in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-084: Generalization studies on CNNs are especially relevant for our work since we focus significantly on equivariance to finite and abelian groups, which can often be realized via periodic convolution.
elements.sent-2411-085: In particular, \cite{graf_measuring_2022 recently established two covering-numbers-based bounds for convolution models.}

elements.section-2411-010: An Overview of Representation Theory and Equivariant Networks
elements.sent-2411-235: We provide a brief overview of some useful concepts from (compact group) Representation Theory in this supplementary section.
elements.sent-2411-236: Associativity: \quad $\forall a, b, c \in G \quad a \cdot (b \cdot c) = (a \cdot b) \cdot c$.
elements.sent-2411-237: Identity: \quad $\exists e \in G: \forall g \in G \quad g \cdot e = e \cdot g = g$.
elements.sent-2411-238: Inverse: \quad $\forall g \in G \ \ \exists g^{-1 \in G: \quad g \cdot g^{-1 = g^{-1 \cdot g = e $.}}}

elements.sent-2411-244: A compact group is a group that is also a compact topological space with continuous group operation.

elements.sent-2411-246: For example, a group can act on functions overs the group's elements: given a signal $x : G \to \R$, the action of $g \in G$ on $x$ is defined as $[g.x](h) := x(g^{-1 h)$, i.e. $g$ "translates" the function $x$.}

elements.sent-2411-264: The classical Fourier analysis of periodic discrete functions can be framed as the representation theory of the $\Z/N\Z$ group.
elements.sent-2411-265: This is summarised by the following result: the regular representation of $G=\Z/N\Z$ is equivalent to the direct sum of all circular harmonics with frequency $k \in \{0, \dots, N-1\}$, i.e. there exists a unitary matrix $\mF \in \Cm^{N \times N}$ such that: \begin{equation}\rho_reg(p) = \mF^* \left(\bigoplus_k \psi_k(p) \right) \mF\end{equation} for $p \in \{0, \dots, N-1\} = \Z/N\Z$ and where $\psi_k$ is the circular harmonic of frequency $k$ as defined earlier in this section.

elements.sent-2411-267: By indexing the dimensions of the vector space $V \cong \Cm^N$ on which $\rho_\text{reg}$ acts with the elements $p \in \{0, \dots, N-1\}$ of $G=\Z/N\Z$, the Fourier transform matrix can be explicitly constructed as \begin{equation}F_{k,p} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \psi_k(p) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} e^{-i k p \frac{2\pi}{N}}\end{equation} One can verify that the matrix is indeed unitary.

elements.sent-2411-274: Given a vector space $V$ associated with a representation $\rho: G \to \GL(V)$ of a group $G$, the group convolution $\vw \gconv \vx \in \Cm^{|G|}$ of two elements $\vx, \vw \in V$ is defined as \begin{equation}\forall g \in G \quad (\vw \gconv \vx)(g) := \vw^T\rho(g)^T \vx \ . \label{def:group_conv}\end{equation} What we defined is technically a group cross-correlation and so it differs from the usual definitions of convolution over groups.

elements.subsection-2411-008: Regarding Norm-based bounds and Other Desiderata of Learning Theory
elements.paragraph-2411-025: Dimension Free and Norm Based Bounds.
elements.sent-2411-535: Dimension-free bounds are interesting because they hint at why the generalization error is not affected by over-parametrizing the model.
elements.sent-2411-536: In this sense, the notion of dimension refers mainly to the input dimension, the number of channels, the width of a layer, and the number of layers.

elements.sent-2411-538: The works of \cite{golowich_size-independent_2018 and \cite{vardi_sample_2022 are two recent examples, with the former work providing an extensive review of other dimension-free bounds.}}
elements.sent-2411-539: Naturally, this is easier to get for larger norms like Frobenius norms, and the question is if we can have similar dimension-free results for smaller norms like spectral or, as we show in the paper, the norm of effective parameters.
elements.sent-2411-540: In the literature around deep learning theory, there are many works around questioning the relevance of classical learning theoretic results, for example using Rademacher complexity, norm-based bounds, and uniform convergence.
elements.sent-2411-541: We can refer for example to works such as \cite{nagarajan_uniform_2019,jiang_fantastic_2020,zhang_understanding_2017,zhu_understanding_2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-542: It is fair to ask if Rademacher complexity analysis, in light of this criticisms, can provide tight bounds for our case.
elements.sent-2411-543: First of all, the preceding works focused on state of the art deep neural networks with large number of layers.
elements.sent-2411-544: Our study is limited to single-hidden layer neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-545: Second, Rademacher complexity is indeed tight for support vector machines \cite{Steinwart2008-jo,shalev-shwartz_understanding_2014,Bartlett2002-gt .}
elements.sent-2411-546: If we fix the first layer of our network and only train the last layer, then we have a linear model, and it is known that training it with Gradient descent with lead to minimum $\ell_2$-norm solutions.
elements.sent-2411-547: For these sub-class of learning algorithms, the analysis boils to previous works on minimum norm classifiers as in \cite{shalev-shwartz_understanding_2014 for which the Rademacher complexity analysis is tight.}
elements.sent-2411-548: Therefore, we believe that Rademacher complexity can still be a relevant tool for shallower models including those discussed in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-549: Previous works also tried to characterize the effect of equivairance on the training dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-550: While this work focuses on the effect of equivariance on optimization, the authors suggest that this implicit bias towards sparse Fourier solutions can be beneficial towards generalization in bandlimited datasets - which is common for non-discrete compact groups such as rotation groups and a common assumption in steerable CNNs.
elements.sent-2411-551: Similar results were also previously obtained in \cite{gunasekar2018implicit for standard convolutional networks, which can be seen as an instance of GCNN with discrete abelian groups.}
elements.sent-2411-552: In this paper, we provided a Rademacher complexity based bound for single-hidden layer equivariant networks.
elements.sent-2411-553: We obtained a lower bound showing the tightness of our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-554: The numerical results showed compelling scaling behaviors for our bound.
elements.sent-2411-555: However, there is still a gap between our bound and the generalization error.
elements.sent-2411-556: Our conjecture is that the gap is related to the idea of implicit bias.
elements.sent-2411-557: We conjecture that stochastic gradient descent training implicitly biases toward only a subset of the hypothesis space even smaller than norm-bounded functions.
elements.sent-2411-558: It is an important next step to tie these two analysis together by characterizing the implicit bias, for example in terms of some kind of norms, and then use Rademacher complexity analysis to find a bound on the generalization error based on the implicit bias.

elements.subsection-2411-011: Dudley's Inequality and Covering Number Bounds
elements.sent-2411-722: Although, for most of the proofs, we try to directly bound the Rademacher complexity, there is a way of bounding it using the covering number of the underlying set.
elements.sent-2411-723: The covering number of a set $\gG$ is the minimum number of balls required to cover the set $\gG$ with the centers within the set, the distances defined according to a metric $d$, and the radius fixed of a given size $\epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-725: The covering can be understood is a way approximating each point in $\gG$ by a set of finite points of $\gG$ with the fidelity $\epsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-726: The following result can be found in many references including \cite{foucart_mathematical_2013,bartlett_spectrally-normalized_2017,Mohri2018-ja .}
elements.sent-2411-727: The idea is to find a bound on the covering number and then use Dudley's inequality to bound the Rademacher complexity.
elements.sent-2411-728: To find a bound on the Rademacher complexity, We start with the following inequality, which is shared across the proofs of some of the other theorems in the paper: \begin{equation}\E_\vepsilon \paran{\sup_{\vu,\vw} \frac 1m \sum_{i=1}^m \eps_i \vu^\top P\circ{\sigma \paran{ \mW\vx_i } }} = \frac{M_1}{m}\E_\vepsilon \paran{\sup_{\vw} \norm{ \sum_{i=1}^m \eps_i P\circ\sigma(\mW\vx_i)}}. \label{ineq:first_peel_off}\end{equation} The above result follows from the Cauchy-Schwartz inequality and peels off the last aggregation layer.
